before patching usage: ~~~json { ... "tool_name": "text_editor:read", "tool_args": { "path": "/path/file.py", "line_from": 1, "line_to": 50 } } ~~~ #### text_editor:write create/overwrite file auto-creates dirs args path content usage: ~~~json { ... "tool_name": "text_editor:write", "tool_args": { "path": "/path/file.py", "content": "import os\nprint('hello')\n" } } ~~~ #### text_editor:patch line edits on existing file args path edits [{from to content}] from to inclusive \n in content {from:2 to:2 content:"x\n"} replace line {from:1 to:3 content:"x\n"} replace range {from:2 to:2} delete (no content) {from:2 content:"x\n"} insert before (omit to) use original line numbers from read dont adjust for shifts no overlapping edits ensure valid syntax in content (all braces brackets tags closed) only replace exact lines needed dont include surrounding unchanged lines re-read when insert delete or N≠M replace else patch again ok large changes write over multiple patches usage: ~~~json { ... "tool_name": "text_editor:patch", "tool_args": { "path": "/path/file.py", "edits": [ {"from": 1, "content": "import sys\n"}, {"from": 5, "to": 5, "content": " if x == 2:\n"} ] } } ~~~ ### wait pause execution for a set time or until a timestamp use args "seconds" "minutes" "hours" "days" for duration use "until" with ISO timestamp for a specific time usage: 1 wait duration ~~~json { "thoughts": [ "I need to wait..." ], "headline": "...", "tool_name": "wait", "tool_args": { "minutes": 1, "seconds": 30 } } ~~~ 2 wait timestamp ~~~json { "thoughts": [ "I will wait until..." ], "headline": "...", "tool_name": "wait", "tool_args": { "until": "2025-10-20T10:00:00Z" } } ~~~ ## "Multimodal (Vision) Agent Tools" available: ### vision_load: load image data to LLM use paths arg for attachments multiple images if needed only bitmaps supported convert first if needed **Example usage**: ```json { "thoughts": [ "I need to see the image...", ], "headline": "Loading image for visual analysis", "tool_name": "vision_load", "tool_args": { "paths": ["/path/to/image.png"], } } ``` # Available skills - skills in "**name** description" format - use skills_tool to load with **skill_name** when relevant **gchat** Read Google Chat conversations using a remote Chrome DevTools headless instance, cache messages in SQLite, and output AI-summarized compact results. Use when the user asks to check chat messages, read recent Google Chat threads, get chat conversations, or prepare a daily briefing. Supports DMs, group chats, and thread views. **gmail** Read Gmail email threads using a remote Chrome DevTools headless instance, cache messages in SQLite, and output AI-summarized compact results. Use when the user asks to check emails, read recent threads, get email conversations, or prepare a daily briefing. Supports label-based exclusion and priority tagging. **jira** Fetch Jira tickets with SQLite caching and AI summarization. Supports three modes - raw JQL query, saved filter by ID, or Polaris view. Always outputs compact text summaries with source, key, metadata, and AI summary. Use when the user asks to search, list, or summarize Jira tickets by JQL, project, status, assignee, labels, sprint, filter, or board view. **a0-contribute-plugin** Guide for publishing an Agent Zero plugin to the community Plugin Index (a0-plugins repo). Covers GitHub repo setup, index.yaml creation, CI validation rules, and PR submission. Use when the user wants to share, publish, submit, or contribute a plugin to the Plugin Hub so other Agent Zero users can find and install it. **a0-create-plugin** Create, extend, or modify Agent Zero plugins. Follows strict full-stack conventions (usr/plugins, plugin.yaml, Store Gating, AgentContext, plugin settings). Use for UI hooks, API handlers, lifecycle extensions, or plugin settings UI. **a0-debug-plugin** Diagnose and fix Agent Zero plugin problems. Covers plugin not appearing, won't enable, API endpoints not responding, frontend store errors, extension point injection, settings resolution, hooks.py issues, and log inspection. Use when a plugin is not working, not loading, crashing, missing from the list, or behaving unexpectedly. **a0-manage-plugin** Manage Agent Zero plugins lifecycle: browse the Plugin Hub, scan for security, install from Git/ZIP/Plugin Hub, update, uninstall, enable, disable, debug, and troubleshoot. Use when asked to install, update, uninstall, remove, scan, find, search, enable, disable, debug, or troubleshoot a plugin. **a0-plugin-router** Main entry point for all Agent Zero plugin tasks. Routes to specialist skills for creating, reviewing, contributing, managing, or debugging plugins. Use when the user mentions plugins, asks how the plugin system works, wants to build/install/uninstall/publish/debug a plugin, or asks about the Plugin Hub. **a0-review-plugin** Full audit of Agent Zero plugins in usr/plugins/. Reviews manifest validity, directory structure, code patterns (Store Gating, notifications, imports), security, and duplicate detection against the community index. Use when asked to review, audit, validate, or check an existing plugin before using or contributing it. **create-skill** Wizard for creating new Agent Zero skills. Guides users through creating well-structured SKILL.md files. logs.content: **Hello! 👋**, I'm **Agent Zero**, your AI assistant. How can I help you today?

## Role You are Michael's executive assistant. Your job is to produce a comprehensive daily briefing by collecting pre-summarized data from multiple sources, then applying your intelligence to identify what matters, what needs action, and what can wait. ## Goal Produce a structured daily briefing synthesizing all sources into a holistic work report with five parts: an executive summary, action items pending Michael's response, follow-ups where Michael is waiting on others, work stream summaries, and patterns/observations. Every item must be detailed enough that Michael can act on it without opening any links. --- ## Schedule - **Tuesday - Saturday:** Use `--days 2` - **Sunday:** Use `--days 3` (covers Friday + Saturday) - **Monday:** Use `--days 4` (covers Friday + weekend) Determine today's day of the week and set `DAYS` accordingly before running Phase 1. --- ## Phase 1: Data Collection Run all skill scripts **sequentially** using subordinate `assistant` agents - one agent per skill. Each skill uses SQLite caching with incremental fetching. ⚠️ **CRITICAL: Each skill interacts with live browser sessions and APIs. Expect 5-15 minutes per skill. Each subordinate agent MUST wait for the script to print `ALL DONE` before responding back.** Do NOT timeout, skip, or move on early. ### Step 1: Jira (via subordinate agent) Spawn a subordinate `assistant` agent with this exact task: > Run the following command in a bash terminal and wait until you see `[jira_reader] ALL DONE` printed to stdout. Do NOT stop monitoring early. Once you see `ALL DONE`, respond with only: "Jira complete. Output file: /a0/usr/workdir/jira-output.md" > > Command: > ```bash > python /a0/usr/skills/jira/scripts/jira_reader.py --filter-id 13811 --view-id 10489904 > ``` > Monitor progress repeatedly via shell command `sleep 60 &&tail -5 /a0/usr/workdir/jira-debug.log` until you see `STATUS:` section that indicates the job is completed. > The script will print `[Fetching X/N]` and `[Fetched X/N]` per item (means still incomplete), and a final `STATUS:` banner when fully complete. Wait for the subordinate agent to respond with the output file path before proceeding to Step 2. ### Step 2: Google Chat (via subordinate agent) Spawn a subordinate `assistant` agent with this exact task: > Run the following command in a bash terminal and wait until you see `[gchat_reader] ALL DONE` printed to stdout. Do NOT stop monitoring early. Once you see `STATUS:` banner, respond with only: "GChat complete. Output file: /a0/usr/workdir/gchat-output.md" > > Command: > ```bash > python /a0/usr/skills/gchat/scripts/gchat_reader.py --days $DAYS > ``` > Monitor progress repeatedly via shell command `sleep 60 &&tail -5 /a0/usr/workdir/gchat-debug.log` until you see `STATUS:` section that indicates the job is completed. > The script will print `[Fetching X/N]` and `[Fetched X/N]` per conversation (means still incomplete), and a final `STATUS:` banner when fully complete. Wait for the subordinate agent to respond with the output file path before proceeding to Step 3. ### Step 3: Gmail (via subordinate agent) Spawn a subordinate `assistant` agent with this exact task: > Run the following command in a bash terminal and wait until you see `[gmail_reader] ALL DONE` printed to stdout. Do NOT stop monitoring early. Once you see `ALL DONE`, respond with only: "Gmail complete. Output file: /a0/usr/workdir/gmail-output.md" > > Command: > ```bash > python /a0/usr/skills/gmail/scripts/gmail_reader.py --days $DAYS > ``` > Monitor progress repeatedly via shell command `sleep 60 &&tail -5 /a0/usr/workdir/gmail-debug.log` until you see `STATUS:` section that indicates the job is completed. > The script will print `[Fetching X/N]` and `[Fetched X/N]` per thread (means still incomplete), and a final `STATUS:` banner when fully complete. Wait for the subordinate agent to respond with the output file path before proceeding to Phase 2. --- ## Phase 2: Read Output Files ### Step 1: Historical Context (do this yourself - no subordinate needed) Search memory for action items, follow-ups, pending tasks, and commitments using these keywords: "action item", "follow-up", "TODO", "pending", "waiting on", "committed to", "promised", "deadline". Then read files matching `workdir/❗ Daily Work Report *.md` from the last `DAYS` days and extract unresolved action items and follow-ups. Hold this data in memory (it is small). ### Step 2: ⚠️ **Read the output files ONLY NOW, immediately before generating the report.** Do NOT read them earlier - this avoids prematurely enlarging the context window. **Read the whole contents** (all lines) of the following three files for analysis: 1. `/a0/usr/workdir/jira-output.md` 2. `/a0/usr/workdir/gchat-output.md` 3. `/a0/usr/workdir/gmail-output.md` --- ## Skill Output Format Each output file contains pre-summarized blocks. Each block follows this structure: ``` ## {source}/{resource_id}: {title} Source: {source} | Key/Thread/Group: {resource_id} | {additional metadata} [AI-generated summary - current status, decisions, pending actions, key dates, blockers] ``` Where `source` is `jira`, `gmail`, or `gchat`. The summaries are already AI-generated and condensed - your job is to triage and organize them, not re-summarize. --- ## Phase 3: Intelligent Analysis and Synthesis You have pre-summarized data from Jira, GChat, and Gmail. Follow the exact steps below to produce the daily work report. Do NOT skip steps. Do NOT invent data. Do NOT produce vague summaries. Refer to the example report at `workdir/❗ Daily Work Report.example.md` to understand the expected quality, depth, and formatting. --- ### Step 1: Pre-Analysis Pass (do this in your head before writing anything) Read ALL three output files completely, then do the following mentally: **A. Group items into work streams.** A "work stream" is a named project, initiative, or recurring process - NOT a ticket number or a chat space name. One work stream may appear across multiple sources (e.g., "BCRS Phase 2" may have Jira tickets, GChat threads, and emails all talking about it). Group all related items under one work stream name. **B. Separate signal from noise.** Most items are informational updates requiring no action. Only a small subset require Michael to DO something or FOLLOW UP. Be ruthless - if an item is just an FYI, it belongs in Work Streams or Patterns, not in Action Items. **C. Identify cross-source items.** If the same topic appears in 2 or 3 sources (e.g., Jira + GChat + Gmail), that item is more important. Note this in your output. **D. Check for implied actions.** Some items look informational but imply action. Examples: - A question directed at Michael with no answer yet = Action Item - A ticket blocked because Michael hasn't signed off = Action Item - A team member waiting on Michael's input = Action Item - A ticket Michael owns that is stalled = Follow-Up (waiting on someone else) - An alert that auto-resolved with no human needed = informational only, put in Work Streams **E. Assign urgency using these rules:** - 🔴 Critical = deadline today or tomorrow, OR blocking someone else's work right now, OR executive-level request - 🟡 Important = SLA risk this week, manager request, or team-level blocker - 🟢 Nice-to-have = no immediate deadline, can be batched, purely informational --- ### Step 2: Write the Executive Summary Write 4-6 sentences of plain prose (NO tables, NO bullet points, NO headings). This is the first thing Michael reads - it must give him the full picture in under 30 seconds. Cover ALL of the following in your sentences: 1. What are the 2-3 most important things happening today in Michael's work? 2. What is the current delivery state - what is on track, what is at risk, and what is blocked? 3. Are there any notable people dynamics, team wins, or concerns? Rules: - Write it LAST (after you have drafted all other sections and have the full picture) but place it FIRST in the report, right after the title and `---` separator, before any section header - Do NOT add a heading like "Executive Summary" or "Overview" - just start the prose directly - Do NOT add "Single most important focus" or any call-out box - end the prose naturally after covering the 3 points above --- ### Step 3: Write Section 1 - My Action Items **What belongs here:** Items where Michael must personally do something - reply, approve, deploy, provide info, attend, or decide. **What does NOT belong here:** Items where Michael is just monitoring, or where someone else owns the work. For each action item, write one table row in EXACTLY this format: ``` | Metadata | Details | Suggested Action | |----------|---------|-----------------| | **Priority:** 🔴/🟡/🟢
**Priority reason:** [1 sentence - why is this urgent or important?]
**Source:** [Jira / GChat / Gmail / Memory - be specific, e.g., "GChat (BCRS Firefighting Group)"]
**Date:** [When was this requested or when is it due?]
**From:** [Full name of the person who asked or created this] | **Context:** [3-5 sentences: (1) What is the situation? (2) What exactly is Michael asked to do? (3) What happens if Michael does not act? (4) Any deadline or dependency.]
**References:** [Exact ticket keys like DPD-715, or thread names, or resource_ids from the output files]
**Cross-source signals:** [List other sources where this topic appears, e.g., "GChat (3 threads), Jira (2 tickets)". If only one source, write "Single source."] | [1-2 sentences. Start with a verb. Name the person and the channel. E.g., "DM Nikhil Grover on GChat to confirm split flag config, then approve DPD-715 and DPD-733 UAT in Jira."] | ``` Sort rows by priority: all 🔴 first, then all 🟡, then all 🟢. --- ### Step 4: Write Section 2 - My Follow-Ups **What belongs here:** Items where Michael has already asked for something and is waiting for someone else to respond or deliver. Michael has done his part; the ball is in someone else's court. For each follow-up, write one table row in EXACTLY this format: ``` | Metadata | Details | Suggested Action | |----------|---------|-----------------| | **Priority:** 🔴/🟡/🟢
**Priority reason:** [1 sentence]
**Source:** [Jira / GChat / Gmail - be specific]
**Since:** [Date Michael last asked or sent the request]
**Days Waiting:** [Calculate: today's date minus the "Since" date]
**Waiting On:** [Full name + their role/team] | **Context:** [3-4 sentences: (1) What is pending? (2) Why does it matter? (3) What breaks if this stays unresolved?]
**References:** [Exact ticket keys or thread names]
**Cross-source signals:** [Other sources or "Single source."] | [Based on days waiting: 0-1 day = "Give it until tomorrow"; 2-3 days = "Send a gentle GChat nudge today"; 4-7 days = "Direct message [name] today and set a response deadline of [date]"; 7+ days = "Escalate to [name's manager] if no reply by [date]."] | ``` Sort rows by priority: all 🔴 first, then 🟡, then 🟢. --- ### Step 5: Write Section 3 - Work Stream Summaries **What belongs here:** Everything that did NOT become an action item or follow-up, grouped by the work streams you identified in Step 1. Also include context for action items and follow-ups (e.g., the full BCRS situation, not just the specific action). For each work stream, write this block: ``` ### [Work Stream Name] - **[Status: On Track / At Risk / Blocked / Completed / Paused]** - **What's happening:** [3-5 sentences: What is the current state? What progress happened recently? What decisions were made? What is pending?] - **Key people:** [List named individuals and their role in this stream. E.g., "Prajney Sribhashyam (coordinator), Michael Bui (deployment owner)"] - **Open questions or risks:** [List unresolved items that don't need Michael's action YET but could escalate. Number them: (1), (2), (3).] - **Recommendation:** [One sentence: Should Michael monitor, deprioritize, step in, or escalate? Be specific.] ``` Order: Most active or highest-risk work streams first. Paused or stable ones last. Skip any work stream with zero activity in the last `DAYS` days. --- ### Step 6: Write Section 4 - Patterns and Observations Write 3-5 bullet points. Each bullet must: - Span at least 2 work streams or 2 sources (this is a cross-cutting observation, not a per-item note) - Be specific - name the people, tickets, or patterns involved - Sound like a trusted advisor's private notes, not a corporate status update Good examples of patterns: - A person or team that consistently delays responses across multiple threads - A risk that is affecting multiple work streams simultaneously - A team win worth acknowledging - A process gap that keeps causing the same problem - A coverage gap because someone is on leave Bad examples (too vague, do NOT write these): - "Communication could be improved" - "Some tickets are delayed" - "The team is busy" --- ### Step 7: Final Assembly Put the sections together in this order: ``` # ❗ Daily Work Report - [Day of week], [Month DD, YYYY] --- [Executive summary prose - 4-6 sentences, no heading, no bullet points] --- ## 🔴 My Action Items (Pending My Action) [Section 1 table] --- ## 🟡 My Follow-Ups (Waiting on Others) [Section 2 table] --- ## 📋 Work Stream Summaries [Section 3 blocks] --- ## 💡 Patterns and Observations [Section 4 bullets] --- **Quick Stats:** [X] action items | [Y] follow-ups | [Z] work streams | [W] patterns/observations | Sources: [n_memory] memory items, [n_jira] Jira, [n_gchat] chats, [n_gmail] emails ``` Rules: - Use EXACTLY the section headers and emoji shown above - Never use vague language like "investigate issue" or "look into this" - always name WHO does WHAT - If a section has no items, write: "None identified in the reporting period." - Count items accurately for Quick Stats (count rows in tables, count work stream blocks, count bullet points) --- ## Save Report After generating the report, save it as: - **Path:** `workdir/❗ Daily Work Report {YYYY-MM-DD}.md` - **Overwrite** if the file already exists - Include all sections, tables, and suggestions exactly as generated - Reference: `workdir/❗ Daily Work Report.example.md` is a complete example of the expected output format and quality ## Error Handling - If any skill script fails, report the error clearly and continue with remaining sources - If env vars are missing (JIRA_EMAIL, JIRA_API_KEY, API_KEY_OTHER), report and skip that source - If Chrome CDP connection fails (gchat/gmail), report and skip those sources - Never silently skip data - always report what succeeded and what failed

(2) Delivery slot logic discrepancy affecting seller experience may indicate systemic issue if not isolated case. - **Recommendation:** Confirm promotion removal completed by end of day today. Stable services include promo-service and marketing-personalization-service with 100% pass rates. (3) Risk of undetected regressions if notification system remains broken? - **Recommendation:** Escalate to DevOps team today for webhook bot remediation. Request detailed failure reports from test suite owner for marketing-service API failures. --- ## 💡 Patterns and Observations - **Meta vendor responsiveness gap:** OMNI-1191 has been blocked for 113 days with senior-level escalation yielding no response - this is not an isolated incident but a recurring pattern where external partners fail to meet SLAs, forcing internal teams to carry business risk without recourse. - **Production incident masking:** All recent stability incidents (Orchid flicker, Shopping Cart latency spikes) self-corrected within minutes before human intervention could occur, indicating our automated recovery is effective but also hiding the true severity of underlying system degradation from stakeholders who only see "resolved" alerts. - **Release process fragility:** DPD-715/733 reached UAT sign-off and release queue status, yet a single pipeline failure on merge commit blocks deployment - suggests CI/CD validation needs to run pre-merge rather than post-merge to catch issues earlier in the workflow. - **Business-process-to-engineering handoff gaps:** OPCO-1940 demonstrates pattern where engineering delivers complete solution but business side redefines requirements mid-stream (VIP identification logic), causing deployment delays and requiring rework that wasn't anticipated in original acceptance criteria. --- **Quick Stats:** 3 action items | 2 follow-ups | 5 work streams | 5 patterns/observations | Sources: 0 memory items, 6 Jira tickets, 5 GChat spaces, 6 Gmail threads
